slides.title: Dan York - Beyond Starlink: Understanding the Coming Waves of LEO Satellite Deployments
Liz Izhikevich - The Limitations of LEO Uplink
Florian Zeiger - Industrial Communication Services via Non-Terrestrial Networks: Requirements and Challenges
Lin Han - Towards Standardized Routing for LEO Satellite Networks: A Comparative Study
Maxime Piraux - Draft update: A Code to Describe Satellite Constellations
Nishanth Sastry - Draft update: A typology of Space Research Infrastructures

chat.author: Nitinder Mohan
chat.text: Morning!

chat.author: Chris Box
chat.text: There's also Viasat/Inmarsat but as they are primarily GEO, I think they rely on partners for adding LEO. And as Dan is explaining, there are many possible partners.

chat.author: Stuart Card
chat.text: So perhaps _the_ big question for us in IETF is how do we make our protocol suite as a whole resilient to the seemingly inevitable Kessler syndrome?

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: "Raise shields..." ?

chat.author: Ali Begen
chat.text: what are the chances of such a satellite coming down and destroying your house?

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: @Ali: Still really really small....

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: there is a good article from IEEE about how many of those ODC are realistic

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: Well, the chances of it destrying my house is small, I don't tknow what the chances of it destrying your house is....

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: blocking spectrum

chat.author: Nitinder Mohan
chat.text: There is quite nice calculator that gives an estimate on the economics of ODC: https://andrewmccalip.com/space-datacenters

chat.author: Ali Begen
chat.text: @Warren, that is a very good answer :)

chat.author: Monika Ermert
chat.text: Interestingly Starlink also filed a big bunch over the German regulator, Bundesnetzagentur

chat.author: Ali Begen
chat.text: is there a rental fee for these satellites? I mean someone charges them for the space they occupy?

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: Starlink needs to file with any country where they have terrestrial stations

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: @Ali: I thin this is more wild west right now. The only managed resource appears to be spectrum

chat.author: Nitinder Mohan
chat.text: Interoperability would be a pipe-dream in this new-age space race

chat.author: Monika Ermert
chat.text: what i meant is the Bundesnetzagentur filed Starlink applications to the ITU

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: As always, inter means giving up your achieved competitive edge for the greater good

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: inter -> interop

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: building million of sats will take many years

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: Yes, 2,000,000 might not happen, but even if <choose your percentage> do, we need to consider the IETF implications (if any).

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: Rick just said what I was trying to, but more eloquently,,,

chat.author: Boris Khasanov
chat.text: @Warren, indeed due to this huge scalability challenge

chat.author: Stuart Card
chat.text: Well said Rick!

chat.author: Corinne Cath
chat.text: i still had a question

chat.author: Corinne Cath
chat.text: especially around the idea that these LEO will be providing connectivity for all

chat.author: Corinne Cath
chat.text: which is not at all the state of the tech right now

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: what about the downlink?

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: There is a related question of affordabiity: Starlink is beyond many people's reach (economically) in many countries

chat.author: Corinne Cath
chat.text: so was wondering the extent to which were ascribing all sorts of very positive effects to shooting all this tech into orbit, when that's not necessarily what the larger business environment is interested in

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: did she said 1:1 ?

chat.author: Kurtis Heimerl
chat.text: 10:1 (its on the slides)

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: thanks

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: I was reading the transcript

chat.author: Dan York
chat.text:
Monika Ermert said:

Interestingly Starlink also filed a big bunch over the German regulator, Bundesnetzagentur

Oh, really?  That's interesting since their "home" regulator would be the US FCC...

chat.author: Marc Blanchet
chat.text: @joerg: "Starlink is beyond many people's reach (economically) in many countries" .  Well, sure, but often there is no alternative, as deployment of 3GPP towers even with radio links is often not considered whenever the density is not enough. So at least they have one alternative now where in many places there was none.

chat.author: Dan York
chat.text:
Joerg Ott said:

There is a related question of affordabiity: Starlink is beyond many people's reach (economically) in many countries

Starlink IS expensive in many countries... but I think it will be interesting to see what Amazon Leo will do on pricing. Amazon is good at making consumer equipment inexpensively (decreasing startup fees) ... and could afford to offer lower costs subscriptions.

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: Right, agreed, but it may be de-facto non-existent

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: It'll be interesting to see how the market develops once competition increases

chat.author: Dan York
chat.text: Yes, I'm very curious to see where this all goes market-wise.

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text:
I have a rural house, where my only option was a WiFi link to a tower, which had a WiFi link to a tower, which had a WiFi link to a tower, which had a WiFi link to a tower which had a T3 to the Internet (in 2024). At night I would get ~12kbps to the Internet. 
With Starlink I actually have real connectivity there...

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: Couldn't agree more -- but your monthly salary is probably substantially higher than the Starlink subscription fee per month. This doesn't hold in many places. Not talking about the developed world

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: @Joerg: Oh, yes, 100% true. Starlink does provide connectivity in many please in the developing world where there is no other option.... but that doesn't actually solve things if people there cannot afford it....

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: I, like everyone else in this room, are indeed in a very privileged position

chat.author: Jorge Amodio
chat.text: how do you account for satellite hand overs?

chat.author: Lou Berger
chat.text: same thing happened in early DOCSIS nets, but they had more channels

chat.author: Tony Li
chat.text: Do we change the protocols to adapt to the link layer?  Or do we improve the link layer?

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: can one really develop more fair Interrnet protocols if one doesn't know what TE is happening in LEO?

chat.author: Ali Begen
chat.text: rather than payinx 2 starlink subscriptions, you better get one subscription from two providers for bonding, apparently it will work better.

chat.author: Kurtis Heimerl
chat.text: I'm a little uncomfortable treating starlink like a black box; they are optimizing their link for the traffic, feels weird to optimize the traffic for link

chat.author: Kurtis Heimerl
chat.text: @tony li yeah exactly

chat.author: Kurtis Heimerl
chat.text: This is all very conceptually similar to cellular systems with mobility and handover

chat.author: Kurtis Heimerl
chat.text: quic and the like already introduced optimizations for these types of conditions

chat.author: Boris Khasanov
chat.text: @Liz - very cool presentation!

chat.author: Warren Kumari
chat.text: @Boris: Yah, that was fascinating...

chat.author: Boris Khasanov
chat.text: @Warren, your comment about 12 Kbps made me think about what we actually have for Internet connectivity...

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: Our low end Internet experiments showed (and people who get EDGE somewhere can probably confirm) that you need way more to get anything sensibly working

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: several hundred kbps and reasonably low latency

chat.author: Stuart Card
chat.text: In early days, I supported a geographic area of 100,000 people with a single DS0 56 kbps line. It all depends on many factors, inc. what you are trying to do and how efficiently you are doing it.

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: and how web content design develops (and people's expectations where the content is created)

chat.author: Joerg Ott
chat.text: I am always amazed that you can't even get sports ticker updates in an app at poor connectivity (plain poor design)

chat.author: Stuart Card
chat.text: @jeorg that is a great example. Long ago, submarines at sea, as a matter of sailor quality of life, got sporting event results over 300 bps VLF links.
